feedback.text: I believe the Digital Fairness Act is a great opportunity to protect the rights of consumers in the EU. There are 2 big issues consumers are facing with regards to digital fairness, that the Act could be expanded to also cover. The first, would be the protection of the ownership of digital goods and content, like software, video games, or other media like music, movies, etc. When you bought a DvD for a movie, a few decades ago, you owned that DvD. If you have a working DvD player, you can still watch that DvD today. The same is true, with regards to games, that came on a disk. If you have the right hardware, you can still play the game. Currently, it is all too common, for companies to sell media to their customers, but then revoking access, or otherwise rendering the product unusable later down the line. This does not make sense. The ECI "Stop Destroying Videogames" is currently trying to tackle this issue with regards to video games, but the issue goes beyond just that particular medium, and extends to all digital content. The digital content sold is still fundamentally the same, the only thing that has changed, is the method of distribution. That should not be a justification to end the concept of ownership. Nobody would accept it, if they bought a toaster, and then the manufacturer could at any point, remotely disable the toaster, without any chance for repair, simply because they stopped selling it. Because it'd be absurd, and an obvious violation of consumer rights. As the world and the way we buy media changes, our laws need to be updated, to reflect that change. They should protect the ownership of digital goods, in the same way that they protect the ownership of tangible goods. The other issue, consumers are currently facing, is the fact that payment processors are abusing their dominant market position, to force digital storefronts to censor themselves. Not to remove illegal content, but rather content that is legal, but that these private companies, have deemed morally objectionable. Sometimes in response to a vocal minority, in a different country, trying to impose their will. A lot of the content targeted is adult content, but there have already been attempts to censor unproblematic content, simply for tackling complex topics. Payment Processors provide a utility. They should process payments on behalf of stores, and customers. They should not, under any circumstances be allowed, to dictate what content stores are allowed to sell. As long as the content is legal, they should be mandated to process the payment for it. It is up to democratically elected officials, and institutions like those of the EU, acting in the interest of the public, to decide what can, and what can't be sold to customers. Not, private companies, with no oversight, that are exploiting their dominant position in the market.
